data.title: Is The War Taking A New Turn? - WTI Technical Analysis
data.description:
By Elior Manier

In war, there are words and realities, propaganda and clearly defined facts - and the frontier between both is rarely so transparent.

President Trump changed the market trajectory after saying US-Iran negotiations could resume - a report initially denied by Iran but later confirmed as Iranian Parliament Speaker Qalibaf travelled to Pakistan, where talks would reportedly occur.

The fact that his diplomatic flight took place with US and Israeli approval proves that the US President wasn't just blowing steam - particularly given Al Arabiya reports that Mojtaba Khamenei, the newly appointed Ayatollah, would also be open to talks.

Nevertheless, the cloud remains over how deep and effective these talks would be regarding an official dropping of Iranian ballistic missiles and nuclear program, and more importantly for immediate markets, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

A ceasefire deal is currently priced at around 44% for the end of April - to me, people are a bit pessimistic about the potential for the war to end sooner rather than later.

The reality, however, gets a bit different, with gas infrastructure attacks, Saudi Arabia leaning to join the war and changing its stance, and the fleet of 4,500 Marines arriving in the Middle East towards the end of the week.

The latter is the most concerning fact for markets, leading to swift comparisons to Afghanistan and Iraq, which also hints at a much longer war.

It will depend, of course, on how negotiations result.

Will it result in free passage in the Strait of Hormuz? Will the Iranian population gain more power and freedom after the 5-week initial wartime period? Will attacks on both sides actually cease and lead to longer-term peace?

All of these questions will have to find clear answers in order for markets to get rid of the uncertainty cloud looming over investors since the beginning of March.

As the situation should become clearer as this week continues, let's dive into a multi-time frame analysis of WTI (US) oil to spot where potential action could take place and where to look if they fail.

US Oil Multi-Time Frame Analysis

WTI Daily Chart

With the intense volatility seen in the commodity since the beginning of the conflict, the daily chart can seem quite unclear.

But traders need to look at what is standing out.

Yesterday's drop tested the 20-Day Moving Average ($86.00), which remains the indicator dictating momentum. Breaching it to the downside would imply further easing in conditions. Above, the action remains relatively bullish.

Also, yesterday's move lower actually brought the action back right around the war spike, implying that the action is at least much more balanced than it was in the past week.

As the morning session continues, bulls are attempting a rebound. Hence, the levels to watch for momentum clearly remain the $93.00-95 zone (bullish above, neutral/bearish below).

WTI 4H Chart and Technical Levels

The 4H RSI is at least not pointing to a further rally from where things stand.

Forming a top-looking shape, bull exhaustion at the key pivot area could forge at least a new range below $93.

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • $92.70 intraday resistance
  • Key momentum pivot $93.00-95 (immediate resistance, bear below)
  • $96.11 4H 50-period MA
  • $98-100 resistance
  • $106-108 June 2022 resistance
  • 2022 and Monday highs $116-120

Support Levels

  • $87-90 mini-support
  • Past session lows $86.49
  • $82.80-84 key support
  • 2025 highs key support $78-80
  • Past-week spike $73.00-74.00
  • $69-70 main support (If ceasefire, should quickly head towards there)
  • 2025 lows $55.00

1H Chart and Action levels

The 1H time frame really shows how yesterday tilted the scales towards a more balanced price action - even slightly bearish.

Yesterday's announcement brought a wave of optimism which quickly found its lows; WTI has been rallying slowly since and forming a rising wedge formation (bearish).

The 1H RSI is also turning lower from neutral, a sign of potential reversal.

But all things considered, as long as bears can't reject the $92.70 intraday resistance, the action is more mixed than anything. Breaking the intraday highs would give the upper hand to the bulls towards $95.

Keep track of the headlines and the wedge support.

Safe Trades!

data.title: A Golden Buying Opportunity: I'm Loading Up While Panic Selling Takes Over
data.description:
After starting out the year on a very strong run, gold (GLD) (IAU) has sold off sharply, especially in the wake of the Iran war.

However, this is exactly the kind of pullback that creates a very attractive buying opportunity from a long-term perspective. The reason I say this is because the sell-off is primarily driven by temporary factors that have nothing to do with gold's long-term fundamental outlook.

In fact, I think that the Iran war itself is actually strengthening the long-term bull case for gold. In this article, I will detail why and share some of the ways that I am taking advantage of the opportunity in gold right now.

Why Gold Is Suddenly Selling Off So Hard

There are three main reasons why gold is selling off right now, and none of them change the long-term story.

First of all, it is expected that extra government spending on the war, as well as supply chain disruptions and the rising cost of oil, will push inflation higher in the near term, therefore reducing the Fed's room to cut rates. As a result, the market is expecting there to be higher-for-longer savings accounts, money market accounts, and CD interest rates, which make them more attractive relative to gold on a short-term basis for a store of value. Given that gold is often compared to real rates, higher interest rates—even if inflation is pushed up a bit as well—are weighing on gold's appeal to short-term-oriented savers.

Meanwhile, another short-term headwind for gold is that the recent market volatility, with numerous stocks pulling back, is likely forcing some investors to liquidate their holdings to prevent or even meet margin calls. Given that gold, after its big run-up and status as a store of value, likely served as a natural source of liquidity during this event, it is probably being sold in some cases to provide cash for these sorts of situations.

Third and finally, the pullback in gold has likely caused a shift in how many momentum traders are playing gold and, therefore, moving from momentum-chasing buyers into momentum-chasing sellers, thereby increasing the sell-off in the short term. However, such momentum-driven moves—both to the upside and the downside—are typically overblown and become significantly detached from fundamentals, thereby creating attractive opportunities for more long-term-oriented investors.

Why The Iran War Is Quietly Supercharging Gold’s Long-Term Bull Case

Importantly, none of these three factors impacts the long-term thesis for owning gold. In fact, the Iran war is advancing many of the key underlying theses driving gold's attractiveness over the long term. For example, wartime spending, with the Pentagon expected to ask for an additional 200 billion dollars for the war, is likely to only further exacerbate an already dire U.S. fiscal situation. In fact, the country was already running a 602 billion dollar deficit in Q1 of fiscal 2026, and this war spending is only going to further exacerbate the problem.

Additionally, the war is threatening global supply chains and the global economy by driving significant energy price volatility and potential disruptions to supply chains and overall trade flows. Moreover, countries like Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan are heavily dependent on oil through the Strait of Hormuz to fuel their economies, and, given that they are such an important trade partner to the United States, this impact on them will likely bleed over into the U.S. as well, on top of the fact that increasing costs in the U.S. are also likely weighing on substantial economic activity there as well. This macroeconomic uncertainty and the growing possibility of an economic slowdown are generally environments where gold outperforms.

Macro Chaos, De-Dollarization, And Why The Market Is Dumping The Best Insurance Policy

Additionally, the heightened geopolitical uncertainty of not knowing whether or not the Iran war is going to further accelerate or potentially even spread into other regions, including growing tensions between the U.S. and China and the U.S. and Russia, also makes gold more attractive as a flight-to-safety hedge. Moreover, it will only further accelerate the de-dollarization trend and put additional pressure on U.S. trade and diplomatic relations with several countries around the world, most importantly with China.

This all bodes well for gold as well. Thus, it appears that the market is selling one of the best insurance investments against deteriorating U.S. finances, global economic disruption, and global geopolitical upheaval, even at a time when all three of those factors are becoming even more pronounced.

How I’m Personally Exploiting This Gold Sell-Off Right Now

So how am I taking advantage of this sell-off? First and foremost, I continue to invest aggressively in direct bullion ownership via popular ETFs (GLDM) and through bullion leases, which pay me a mid-single-digit yield while retaining full ownership of my gold bullion and not having to pay any storage fees or expense ratios. Additionally, I'm taking advantage of the recent sharp pullback in gold miners (GDX) (GDXJ) by identifying some deeply undervalued dividend-paying mining stocks.

The future is uncertain, and it is entirely possible that gold continues to sell off aggressively for a while yet, which will likely bring miners lower with it. However, the long-term fundamentals for gold have only become more attractive with the onset of the Iran war, not less attractive. Therefore, I view this sell-off as a huge gift to gold investors, and I'm taking advantage of it accordingly to further enhance my 7–8% yielding below-market beta and total return outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: I'm Buying These 10-11% Yields For Recurring Income
data.description:
Having a good margin of safety is a good mantra to live by when it comes to income investing. In the words of Warren Buffett, “When you build a bridge, you insist that it can carry 30,000 pounds, but you only drive 10,000-pound trucks across it.”

Sleeping well at night means that you don’t overpay for a stock. What’s even better is if the stock pays you a handsome yield for owning it, which further de-risks your initial principal.

This brings me to the following two high-yielding picks, both of which carry decent margins of safety and have strong dividend track records, offering the best of both worlds. Let’s explore what makes each a compelling ‘Buy’ for value and income!

No. 1: Ares Capital – 10.5% Yield

Ares Capital (ARCC) is the largest BDC by asset size and is externally managed by Ares Management (ARES). ARES is a global asset manager with $623 billion in AUM, of which $407 billion is tied to private credit. ARES’ broad credit market expertise helps to ensure that ARCC has a pipeline of vetted deal flow.

ARCC’s investment portfolio is primarily comprised of first-lien senior secured debt, which comprises 69% of portfolio total when including its stake in Ivy Hill Asset Management. Software represents 24% of portfolio total, followed by Healthcare at 11% and Commercial Services at 9%.

The average portfolio company investment represents just 0.2% of total. As shown below, top 15 investments represent just one quarter of the portfolio and the remaining 75% is comprised of 588 companies.

ARCC demonstrated solid performance during Q4 2025, generating a return on equity of over 10% and NII per share of $0.50. This more than covered the $0.48 quarterly dividend rate. ARCC has remarkably paid an uninterrupted and overall growing dividend since 2009.

While NAV per share declined slightly by 0.35% sequentially, it’s up by 0.25% from a year ago. As shown below, ARCC’s NAV per share has meaningfully risen from $16.50 10 years ago to $19.94, all while paying out a rising dividend over this timeframe.

ARCC’s credit metrics remain solid, with non-accruals at just 1.8% of cost, sitting well below the 2.5% historical average. Portfolio company interest coverage ratio improved to 2.2x while leverage declined modestly during Q4. Moreover, average company EBITDA grew by 9% YoY and organic growth rate significantly outperformed GDP and the broadly syndicated loan market.

ARCC is positioned to build upon its record $15.8 billion in new investments last year. This is driven by a sharp acceleration in M&A activity in the second half of last year. The increased demand for borrowing is coming from both existing borrowers and new borrowers, of which 100 were added last year.

The recent higher redemption activity from retail investors in the private credit space could also benefit ARCC as it could result in less competition from private credit players like Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL). While "SaaS-pocalypse" has made headlines as of late due to AI disruption risk, management has highlighted the strength of its software investments with conservative loan-to-value ratios of 37% on average, as noted during the recent earnings call:

The No. 1 risk that we identified in the software space was technology risk and obsolescence risk. And so, we said to ourselves, if we're going to have a thesis in the space and build a book, we really want to make sure that every single software company we put in the portfolio is highly resistant to technology risk.

And obviously, AI is probably the most disruptive technology risk that we could have imagined. And it absolutely is going to disrupt a lot of software companies, and I don't want to sugarcoat it. But we still believe strongly that we've constructed a portfolio that will remain highly resistant to this risk.

ARCC maintains a solid balance sheet with BBB and equivalent credit ratings from all three ratings agencies. This is supported by a safe debt to equity ratio of 1.08x, sitting below its 1.25x target, and over $6 billion in liquidity.

I find solid value in ARCC at the current price of $18.28 with a 10% discount from NAV. While ARCC is far from the cheapest in the BDC sector, it doesn’t have to be, considering its strong track record of NAV/share growth and dividends.

As shown below, ARCC’s P/NAV valuation currently sits at one of its lowest points over the past 10 years except for the outlier in 2020. At the current price, investors get to collect an appealing 10.5% yield with a solid margin of safety.

No. 2: Starwood Property Trust – 11.0% Yield

Starwood Property Trust (STWD) is a sizable commercial mortgage REIT that has a diversified investment platform. This includes commercial loans, residential and infrastructure finance, property ownership, and loan servicing. Having a hybrid model means that STWD can invest in attractive opportunities across segments while de-risking the portfolio from dependence on any one segment.

STWD has a $30.7 billion asset base, of which commercial loans comprise 54%. As shown below, U.S. office represents just 8% of the portfolio. Residential & Infrastructure Lending and Owned Properties make up another 18% and 19% of the portfolio, as shown below.

STWD generated distributable earnings of $0.42 during Q4 2025, which is below the $0.48 quarterly dividend rate. However, the dividend would have been fully covered when adjusting for timing-related factors, which could have resulted in DE per share of $0.49.

Timing factors include a major ramp-up of STWD’s owned properties portfolio with the acquisition of a net lease platform, which will contribute to earnings in future quarters. Moreover, STWD has elevated cash balance due to repayments. Management views this as temporary as cash is deployed, including $2.5 billion worth of capital deployments during Q4 alone, as noted during the recent earnings call:

The second timing issue was higher-than-normal cash balances, which led to $0.04 of reduced earnings. We completed 3 securitizations in the quarter, one in each of commercial lending, infrastructure lending and net lease that combined created incremental proceeds of $290 million.

We also continued to shift secured debt to unsecured debt, issuing $1.1 billion of high yield in the quarter and executed a takeout refinancing on part of our affordable multifamily portfolio, which generated cash of $240 million in late September and October. All of this cash will ultimately be a source of incremental DE as it gets deployed into new investments across our diversified cylinders.

Looking ahead, declining interest rates compared to a year ago is a tailwind for STWD as this could drive higher refinancing and increased loan originations. The lower rates could also support higher property valuations.

STWD’s real estate fundamentals are supported by sharply declining multifamily supply, which could lead to stronger rent growth. At the same time, management continues to de-risk the portfolio, with office property loans representing just 8% of the portfolio. Plus, the Infrastructure Lending segment is seeing strong demand tied to energy consumption and data center expansion.

STWD maintains a sound balance sheet with a debt-to-undepreciated equity ratio of 2.4x, which is more than a full turn below that of peer Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT). I also see value in STWD at the current price of $17.39 with a price-to-undepreciated book value of 0.9x, giving investors a significant margin of safety.

Meanwhile, investors are paid an attractive 11% dividend yield at the current price, which sits toward the high end of its five-year range, as shown below.

Risks to Consider

Risks to ARCC include lower interest rates, considering its mostly floating rate debt portfolio. This could be offset by interest rate floors and lower cost of borrowing. A deteriorating economic climate due to higher cost of oil could lead to pressures on borrowers’ financial health and their ability to make interest payments.

Risks to STWD include credit risks as it relates to the remaining office portfolio loans. Remaining resolutions on REO (real estate owned) properties could see delays, which could impact timing of capital deployment. Change in economic conditions could also see reduced demand for loans.

Investor Takeaway

Ares Capital and Starwood Property Trust both stand out as having long dividend track records and a significant discount to book value. ARCC has a highly diversified portfolio of senior secured loans to middle-market companies. It’s backed by strong credit metrics and a long track record of covering and growing its dividend.

STWD’s hybrid real estate lending and investment platform spreads risk across commercial, residential, and infrastructure segments. It’s expected to see earnings growth as the newly acquired net lease portfolio ramps up and as capital is deployed.

Together, ARCC and STWD investors are getting paid well to wait. Their risk is managed through diversification, underwriting discipline, and scale advantages, making them solid "Buys" for high income at present.

data.title: Why Gold In 2026? An Anchor For Indian Portfolios
data.description:
Strong macros, soft returns

India’s macroeconomic credentials remain strong, with the country continuing to post one of the highest growth rates among major economies - above 7% for three consecutive years1 - despite persistent global headwinds. Growth has been upheld by resilient domestic demand, public investment, and policy initiatives.

However, these favourable macro fundamentals have not translated into financial market performance. The INR has weakened, capital inflows have been subdued, and asset returns have been relatively muted. Gains in Indian equities have moderated amid elevated valuations, while monetary easing2 has compressed yields on debt instruments, making them less attractive for new investors. Against this backdrop, gold stands out as a notable outperformer (Chart 1).

Chart 1: Gold in the lead

Returns of key assets in 2025 and y-t-d in INR*

While the broad-based strength of economic activity suggests growth momentum is likely to persist, risks to the outlook stem largely from external uncertainties – spillover from geopolitical tensions, volatility in the global financial markets, and uncertain global economic prospects, all of which have intensified since the beginning of 2026. The implications of the ongoing West Asian conflict are particularly significant for India,3 from energy supplies to trade and capital flows. Prolonged disruption could exert pressure on inflation, the INR, and sectoral performance.

Gold has emerged as one of the strongest performing assets in recent years, outperforming equities, bonds, and currencies as investors have sought protection against geopolitical tensions, policy uncertainty, and inflation risks. Price momentum has further reinforced investor demand. Importantly, many of the drivers behind this rally remain firmly in place in 2026.

In this environment, building strong buffers and constructing resilient portfolios becomes increasingly important for wealth preservation. The case for gold in Indian portfolios extends beyond recent performance. Market cycles, correlation dynamics, currency movements, and periods of systemic stress all reinforce gold’s role as a portfolio stabiliser.

Gold in the equity cycle

The relationship between equities and gold often reflects shifts in market sentiment. The equity-gold ratio (Nifty–gold ratio) captures the relative performance of the two assets over time (Chart 2). Periods of strong equity market performance have been associated with elevated ratios, while episodes of financial stress and equity drawdowns have coincided with declines, as gold outperforms. The ratio has recently fallen to around 1.5-1.6, well below its long-term average of 3, reflecting gold’s strong relative performance amid heightened macroeconomic and financial market uncertainty. Such phases typically signal a shift in investor preference towards safer assets and underscore gold’s role as a stabilising allocation within portfolios. And while we believe such a level is consistent with the current uncertain environment, there is also a potential for reversal.

Chart 2: Shifting from risk to safety

Nifty and MCX gold spot price ratio*

Systemic buffer

Gold has historically shown strong resilience during episodes of systemic risk, often delivering positive returns while limiting portfolio losses. With few exceptions, its performance during such periods has helped reduce overall portfolio drawdowns (Chart 3).

Chart 3: Gold during systemic stress

Stocks, bonds and gold during various crises*

Changing nature of diversification

Diversification benefits of traditional asset classes have come into question in recent years. The relationship between equities and bonds - key components of most portfolios - has shifted, with the two increasingly moving in tandem. Historically, Indian government bonds tended to exhibit a negative correlation with equities, helping balance portfolio risks. However, over the past few years, the equity-bond relationship has turned increasingly positive (Chart 4). In contrast, gold has continued to predominantly display a negative correlation with equities across most periods, reinforcing its role as an effective diversifier during episodes of market stress.

Chart 4: Limits of bond diversification

Correlation equities vs bonds and gold*

Currency hedge

Movements in domestic gold prices reflect global gold market dynamics, import duty, and exchange rate movements. With gold priced internationally in USD, the value of the Indian rupee can significantly influence its domestic price. Periods of INR depreciation tend to amplify gold price rises (Chart 5). Over longer horizons, the depreciation of the rupee has therefore contributed meaningfully to gold’s return profile. This becomes increasingly relevant during periods of stress, when capital flows and exchange rates tend to be volatile. During such times, gold provides additional protection against uncertainty as well as from currency risk within portfolios.

Chart 5: INR weakens, gold rises

Gold prices and USD-INR indexed*

Strategic asset – gold in portfolios

Gold plays an important role as a strategic allocation within diversified portfolios. Beyond its resilience during periods of uncertainty, gold has delivered long-term returns across economic cycles. Our analysis shows that gold complements equities and broad-based portfolios by providing a unique combination of returns and diversification. These characteristics help improve portfolio resilience by enhancing risk-adjusted returns, reducing volatility, and limiting drawdowns.

Our portfolio analysis further highlights gold’s positive contribution. Over a 19-year period4, an average INR portfolio would have delivered higher risk-adjusted returns and lower drawdowns with gold allocations in the range of 7.5% to 15%, underscoring its value as a core portfolio component (Chart 6).

Chart 6: Portfolio returns with gold

Risk-adjusted returns of a hypothetical portfolio with and without gold *

Conclusion

An environment characterised by elevated geopolitical tensions, shifting market correlations, and persistent currency risks underscores the importance of building resilient portfolios. Gold’s performance across market cycles, its diversification attributes, and its ability to provide protection during periods of financial stress reinforce its strategic, long-term relevance within portfolios. For Indian investors in particular, gold offers an additional layer of resilience by amplifying returns during periods of rupee depreciation.

Footnotes

1Press note on new series of GDP estimates, MoSPI 27 February, 2026.

2RBI has cut policy rates by 125bps since 2025.

3Why India looks especially vulnerable as conflict rages in Middle East, CNBC, 2 March 2026. Beyond Oil: How US-Iran war and Middle East crisis may hit India’s economy, Times of India, 5 March 2026.

4End 2006 to end 2025.

data.description:
Executive summary

  • The Fund returned -0.06% (IS Share Class) during Q4 2025 vs. 2.19% for the Russell 2000 ® Index.
  • Stock selection within the healthcare and the consumer staples sectors detracted from the Fund's relative performance.
  • Our beliefs remain that speculation has pushed prices for AI-related businesses to perfection; if the shift continues away from these companies in the new year, our portfolio is positioned to take advantage of a swing towards earnings, cash flows, and other quality fundamentals that drive stocks in the long term.

Market Environment

The speculation that defined 2025 hit a crescendo in October before a sentiment shift cooled the AI trade somewhat as the year drew to a close. This shift also allowed value to beat growth in small caps for the fourth quarter, though falling just short for the full year. While it is much too soon to call the end of market speculation, it is refreshing that the discourse around AI has become less euphoric and potentially leading investors to evaluate options more critically, offering opportunities for some beaten-down sectors to stand out.

Performance Review

While the heavy speculation from Q3 continued into October, we witnessed a shift in sentiment later in the fourth quarter. Some of the “bubbly” AI businesses moved lower, and value-oriented segments of the market began to attract some capital. The one big exception to the downshift in speculation was the biotech and pharmaceutical space, which enjoyed strong fourth quarter returns.

data.title: MKS Inc.: Long-Term Structural Growth From Rising Semi And PCB Complexity
data.description:
Introduction

MKS Inc. (MKSI) shares have pulled back 7% since their highs in February on the back of the recent correction stemming from the US-Iran War. I think that this creates an attractive BUY opportunity to accumulate into MKSI's shares based on a few key reasons. Although MKSI's shares are up 166% YoY, I think there is still upside to the stock, given that it is still trading at 22.7x forward FY2026 P/E, which is still a significant discount to its peers, which are trading at 30-40x.

There are several key growth drivers that MKSI is seeing in its business: First, MKSI is benefitting from the overall growth in industry WFE spending, which is driven by the migration to advanced logic. Second, MKSI is benefitting from the rising layer count in PCBs, which will help grow its PCB equipment and chemistry business. Lastly, MKSI is seeing an improving outlook in its legacy Specialty Industrial Market, which is expected to grow YoY in 2026 compared to the declines we have seen in the past 2 years.

Business Overview

MKSI is a critical provider of subsystems for semiconductor equipment; its products are used in most of the major semiconductor processing steps, such as deposition, etching, cleaning, lithography, etc. Most of the well-known WFE providers - such as Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX), ASML (ASML), KLA Corp (KLAC) utilize MKSI's subsystems. The company's broad subsystem offering allows them to address 85% of the needs of the WFE market.

Besides semiconductor equipment, MKSI also provides equipment vital for the production of PCBs and packaging substrates, such as laser drilling systems, electronics chemistries, and plating equipment. This has been MKSI's fastest-growing segment in 2025, mainly driven by the increasing layer count in PCBs in AI applications.

MKSI also provides chemicals and equipment in Specialty Industrial Market. This ranges across multiple applications, such as Industrial (automobile & solar manufacturing), Life and Health Science (Bioimaging, Medical Instrument Sterilization, etc.), Research and Defense (Universities, Laboratories and Government). To me, this is the least interesting segment of MKSI, as it represents mainly legacy products that have limited growth drivers.

Currently, MKSI sources 43% of its revenue from semiconductor subsystems (semiconductor segment), 28% of its revenue from PCB-related equipment/chemicals (Electronics and Packaging), and 29% of its revenue from Specialty Industrial.

Figure 1: MKSI Revenue Breakdown in 2025

Beneficiary of Rising WFE Spending

WFE spending has been rising over the past few years; the migration of advanced logic from 5nm to 3nm to 2nm, requires incrementally more process steps across all semiconductor fabrication processes (etch, deposition, lithography, cleaning). This is what has been driving the growth in WFE spending, which has grown an impressive 13.7% YoY in 2025 (to $133 billion). According to SEMI, this trend is expected to continue going into 2026 and 2027, with total WFE spending expected to grow 8-9% over the next 2 years.

Even if the AI megatrend slows down, I don't think that the migration to advanced logic will stop. Major customers, even non-AI customers, such as Apple, have already committed to 2nm capacity at TSMC. TSMC is currently building out 100,000 wafers per month capacity for its 2nm process node in 2026, and they would have to bring in shipments of WFE before the wafer mass production ramp.

Figure 2: SEMI Total Semiconductor Equipment Market Size from 2023-2027

MKSI's largest customers for the semiconductor subsystems segment are Applied Materials and Lam Research. Although the company does not disclose the revenue contributions from these two companies currently, prior to the Atotech acquisition, these two companies used to contribute 27% of MKSI's revenue in 2021. Going forward, MKSI will be able to ride on the back of the growth of these two companies. According to consensus estimates, Applied Materials and Lam Research are expected to grow by 10% and 20% in revenue, respectively, which should drive order momentum for MKSI's semiconductor segment. Other major customers like ASML and KLA are also expected to post similar growth, which will be the key growth driver for MKSI considering that the semiconductor segment is the group's largest revenue contributor.

Rising Layer Counts in AI PCBs

The most attractive business for MKSI, however, is actually its Electronics and Packaging Segment. While the group's overall revenue grew 10% YoY in 2025, the Electronics and Packaging Segment grew at double the corporate average at 20% YoY. AI has been the primary reason behind this growth - compared to traditional applications, which use PCBs with single-digit to low teens layer counts, high-end AI applications often require PCBs with layer counts above 20.

Figure 3: AI Server PCB and CCL Specification

Similar to the situation for advanced logic, the migration to higher and higher layer count PCBs needs incrementally more PCB manufacturing and chemistry equipment, which has been driving the growth of this segment. But I think the most attractive part of this business is its chemical sales; when MKSI sells equipment for PCBs (chemistry equipment), PCB manufacturers also have to make recurring purchases for chemicals to make these machines work. According to management, for every $100 million in equipment sold, $20-40 million in annual recurring chemical revenue is generated. This means that MKSI's revenue will grow each year, as long as its installed base is continuously growing.

For the past 2 years, MKSI has been seeing record levels for its chemistry equipment sales, which will translate to a growing level of chemistry revenue down the line. Based on MKSI's latest earnings call, AI-related applications account for 10% of chemistry sales (Materials Solutions Division), which translates to about 1,323 million x 10% = 132 million in AI-related chemistry revenue. To the group, this translates to about 4% of FY2025 revenue, which seems small. But don’t forget (1) MKSI's chemistry revenue has higher than corporate average margins - which implies greater contribution at the dollar profit level. (2) This does not include equipment and semiconductor subsystem sales related to AI. So the group's AI exposure should be much larger. Going forward, management is expecting this ratio to only continue to grow, driven by AI-related applications.

Better-Than-Feared Legacy Segment Performance

While MKSI's semiconductor/PCB-related business is booming, you might be concerned about MKSI's legacy business (Specialty Industrial) - especially since this segment is exposed to the traditional automotive segment, which is suffering from significant industry headwinds. This concern is valid considering that this segment has been on the decline since 2024. Throughout the past two years, we have been seeing a continuous mid-single decline for the Specialty Industrial segment, mainly because of the soft automotive industry.

While the automotive industry will remain weak heading into 2026, we have seen a gradual improvement in this segment in Q4 2025. For the first time in 2025, the Specialty Industrial segment posted some slight growth YoY during this quarter. Going forward, management is expecting growth in Research and Defense to help offset the decline we are seeing from this segment. Overall, they are expecting slight growth for the Specialty Industrial in 2026 because of this. To me, since the revenue contribution from this segment is reducing over time, as long as there is no significant decline in this segment, it would not drag down overall growth for the group significantly.

Financials

The primary concern about MKSI's Financials is its current debt position. As of Q4 2025, the company has a net debt position of $3,603 million (Debt $4,278 million - Cash & Cash Equivalent of $675 million). You might be wondering why MKSI has so much debt on hand. This was mainly from its Atotech acquisition in 2022 for $6.5 billion, which has now transformed into the Materials Solution Division today. While this acquisition may seem expensive, I feel that it was strategically beneficial to MKSI. Through this segment, MKSI is able to generate recurring chemistry sales, which helps to cushion the cyclicality of the WFE/PCB manufacturing equipment business.

But near-term, MKSI is not expected to face any liquidity risk - the company has $1.4 billion in immediate liquidity, which is sufficient to cover any near-term debt obligations. The current debt level is also manageable at the moment, as MKSI has an interest coverage of about 2.33 and a Debt/Equity ratio of 1.62, which indicate moderate levels of debt. Although this diminishes our investment case slightly, given that the company is mainly focused on deleveraging, I think that the company's operational improvements over the next few years will be able to significantly reduce the burden of its debts.

Figure 4: MKSI Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Profile

Valuation

Currently, MKSI is trading at a forward FY2026 P/E of 22.7x, which is much cheaper than its peers. This is because MKSI is not a pure WFE play due to its PCB manufacturing/chemistry business. Its WFE peers, such as Lam Research, Applied Materials, and KLA, are trading at a much higher valuation of between 32x and 43x. The market cap of these peers is also significantly larger, as MKSI is focused on selling semiconductor subsystems rather than full semiconductor systems. But the current valuations for MKSI are unfair, as looking at consensus EPS growth forecasts, MKSI's growth is in line with or better than its WFE peers, yet the stock is trading at a cheaper valuation. Against Entegris (ENTG), which is also in the chemicals business, MKSI is also trading at a relative discount, which reinforces my point. I have also included DuPont in the analysis as it is also a chemicals business, but since the business is dominated by legacy applications, I think that it would not be fair to compare the two.

Figure 5: MKSI Peer Analysis and Key Metrics, Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

Company Market Cap (USD) EPS (USD) P/E (2026) EPS Growth (2026)
MKS Inc 15,300 9.9 22.7 25.5%
Lam Research 285,000 5.3 42.6 28.2%
Applied Material 283,000 11.1 32.2 17.3%
KLA 196,000 36.6 40.7 9.9%
Entegris 17,400 3.4 33.5 24.0%
DuPont 17,400 2.3 18.4 36.8%

Risk to the Company

There are several key risks that could materially affect the BUY thesis. Although I am not expecting these scenarios to happen in the Base Case of my analysis, they could pose a material downside if they occur. The most major would be a potential slowdown in AI Capex. Currently, the demand for advanced logic and memory is driven by AI-related spending by the top 4 Hyperscalers. If there is a significant slowdown in AI Capex, this could be harmful to the overall WFE spending by both logic and memory foundries.

Another key risk would be the high concentration of MKSI's semiconductor revenue in several key customers. The most important customers for MKSI would be Lam Research, Applied Materials, ASML, and KLA. If there is a significant loss of share allocation from these key customers, it could drag the group's overall growth.

Lastly, MKSI also possesses a moderate degree of financial risk. MKSI carries debt stemming from its past acquisition of Atotech; if there is a material deterioration in MKSI's ability to generate operating profit/cash flows, this could result in a potential default scenario for MKSI.

Conclusion

Based on the aforementioned reasons, I think that the market's current assessment of MKSI's valuation is unfair. The company is clearly benefiting from the overall increase in WFE spending and, on top of that, is also benefiting from its PCB/chemistry business, which is growing at an even faster pace. Yet, MKSI's current 22.7x forward FY2026 P/E is a significant discount compared to its high-flying WFE and Chemical industry peers, which generally trade above 30x P/E. This could be because of the higher levels of debt that MKSI has. But I don't think that the value penalty is fair, as the debt level is manageable and will be less of an issue as MKSI grows in operating scale. Hence, I initiate coverage of MKSI with a Buy Rating, as I think that the risk-reward is much better than its peers.

data.title: Not All Hope Is Lost: Maintaining Super Micro Computer With A Strong Buy
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) shed over 33.3% on Friday after disappointing the market, yet again.

Three Supermicro employees, including one of its co-founders, were indicted by the DOJ for allegedly violating the Export Control Reform Act. The government accused them of smuggling around $2.5 billion worth of servers containing Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to China, which, as we know, is a big no-no. The stock was initially down around 2% on Monday but later recovered and closed up over 5% as investors started buying the pullback.

The indictment charged Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Ruei-Tsang “Steven” Chang, and Ting-Wei “Willy” Sun with engaging in a conspiracy using false documents and staged dummy servers to illegally ship billions of dollars' worth of servers and GPUs to China. SMCI noted that the company itself has not been named as a defendant.

Liaw was one of SMCI’s original founders back in 1993 and had been sitting on its board since 2023. Chang ran sales out of the company's Taiwan office, and Sun worked as an outside contractor.

This is not SMCI’s first rodeo with the law. Days after the Hindenburg report, SMCI announced it would be late filing its annual 10-K with the SEC, citing the need to review its internal controls over financial reporting. Then came the real gut punch: its auditor, Ernst & Young, resigned, which was a rare and alarming move that sent the stock into freefall. Since then, the stock hasn’t seen a good day.

Shortly after, reports surfaced that the DOJ had opened a formal investigation, and Nasdaq sent SMCI a noncompliance letter, raising the real possibility of delisting. At one point, the stock had shed over 80% from its all-time highs. Now, it is down around 83% from all-time highs.

The company eventually filed its overdue financial statements in February 2025, narrowly avoiding delisting. And yet, through all of this, Wally Liaw, who had previously resigned from the company in 2018 amid an earlier accounting scandal, was not only still at the company but sitting on its board. That detail, in hindsight, is hard to overlook.

Stock performance and past coverage

The stock has been through it, and it doesn’t take looking at the chart to know that. While SMCI is up 427% over the past five years, almost every other timeframe leaves the stock in deep red territory. The stock is down over 47.4% over the past year, down another 30% year to date, down 56% over the past 6 months, and down another 35% over the past month.

With the stock down over 83% from all-time highs and the worst being priced in on Friday, I am maintaining the stock with a strong buy. My last strong buy from late January isn’t aging well, especially after the 33% drop on Friday. And honestly, I’m not going to say I saw this coming.

And yet the underlying business remains genuinely compelling. SMCI is a top supplier of AI infrastructure at a moment when demand for that infrastructure has never been higher. It showcased new Blackwell- and Vera Rubin-based servers at Nvidia GTC just last week. Management has guided for $40 billion in revenue for FY26. None of that goes away because of the news.

My investment thesis then rests on a clean separation between the alleged misconduct of a few individuals and the operational health of the business itself. That's a bet on the company's ability to rebuild trust with regulators, with customers, and with the market. Given the history, that's not a small ask.

I am maintaining the stock with a buy as I think the worst is behind us at current levels. With the stock recovering on Monday, buyers have stepped in. Still, before the RSI goes back to neutral to bullish territory (as it's oversold at 27 now), I would recommend investors watch closely. I’m not expecting the stock to break above its EMAs in the short term, but I do expect momentum to push the stock above the mid-20 level.

Valuation

Valuation isn’t a point of concern now, nor was it back when I last covered the stock in late January. Compared to the peer group, SMCI is looking the most attractive now from a valuation perspective.

The valuation case for SMCI is difficult to ignore, as seen below. Trading at a forward EV/Sales of just 0.32x, compared to 1.12x for (HPE) and 0.87x for (DELL), the market is essentially pricing in near-zero premium for what is arguably the most AI-exposed server business of the three. The forward P/E of 11.12x sits well below Dell's 14.54x and less than half of HPE's 21.58x, while the non-GAAP forward PEG of 0.35 signals that the market isn't giving SMCI any credit for its growth trajectory.

The forward EV/EBITDA of 6.83x is roughly in line with HPE at 6.72x, which is striking given that SMCI is growing at a dramatically faster rate. So, if you strip away the governance concerns and look purely at the numbers, SMCI is priced like a broken company, and that's either a massive opportunity or a value trap, depending entirely on whether management can finally get out of its own way.

What’s next

The near-term roadmap for SMCI is a legal and regulatory one. Beyond the courtroom, the company's next earnings report is due May 5th. That print will matter, not just for the numbers, but for what management says on the call. Investors will want to hear a credible plan for strengthening compliance and governance, and so will I. On the business side, the key question is customer retention.

Longer term, the AI infrastructure buildout remains the rising tide that could lift SMCI despite itself. Demand for Blackwell- and Vera Rubin-based systems is real, and SMCI's liquid cooling technology gives it a genuine edge in next-generation data center deployments.

data.title: How AI Can Lift Manufacturing Out Of Its Productivity And Staffing Squeeze
data.description:
By Edse Dantuma, Senior Sector Economist, Industry and Healthcare

Manufacturing risks missing out on major productivity gains

Larger manufacturing companies use AI three times as often as smaller companies

AI adoption is strongly related to company size. Larger manufacturing companies with 250 or more employees use AI three times as often (namely 64% of the total) as smaller companies with 10 to 50 employees (21%). Although fast-growing AI frontrunners are often small startups or scale-ups, scale itself appears to be the differentiating factor for most mature manufacturing companies. Across the industry, hundreds of AI use cases have now been identified, and most surveyed manufacturing companies expect a positive return within one to four years.

EU manufacturing is increasingly using AI, but is still lagging behind other sectors

EU manufacturing companies are increasingly incorporating AI into their business processes. In 2025, 17% of manufacturers used AI, two and a half times as many as in 2023. The share of ‘heavy users’– companies using at least three AI technologies – quadrupled over the same period to 6%. But the downside is clear: laggards are struggling to catch up with the frontrunners. And with average AI use across EU sectors now at 20%, compared with 17% in manufacturing, the industry still has some ground to make up.

Belgium and Denmark are the industrial AI leaders within the EU

Industrial AI adoption varies widely across the EU. While fewer than one in ten manufacturing companies in Romania and Poland use AI, the figure rises to one in three in Sweden and Austria. In other Northwest European countries such as Belgium and Denmark, it’s closer to two in five. Despite these differences, AI adoption is accelerating rapidly across the EU. In almost every EU country, the number of manufacturing companies using AI has more than doubled in the past two years.

Industrial investment in software is growing rapidly in the EU...

Productive AI deployment requires larger software investment. In the larger EU countries for which data is available, industrial investment in software and databases has increased significantly over the past 10 years. While the value of software and databases owned by manufacturing companies increased sharply – by 33% between 2013 and 2018 – due to additional investment, growth accelerated even further between 2018 and 2023 (the last year for which data is available for nine larger EU countries), exceeding 50%. This is a very strong increase comparable to the average software capital growth in all sectors. This particularly stands out when compared to the 5.5% growth in total capital goods in the manufacturing industry and the growth of only 2.5% in ownership of computer hardware.

…but German manufacturing needs to catch up to harness the potential of AI

In some countries, investments in industrial software are clearly lagging behind. This is particularly true for the Netherlands, where software ownership fell by 9% between 2018 and 2023. While there is growth in Germany and Italy (+8% and +17%, respectively), it lags far behind the average growth of 51%. The Netherlands does start from a high level, however. In 2018, the Dutch manufacturing industry had the second-largest software ownership relative to the sector's added value. This is not the case for Germany. In fact, as early as 2013, the European industrial powerhouse had the lowest software ownership among the larger EU countries, and this remained the case in 2024. The added value of the German manufacturing industry declined relatively sharply during this period, which may explain the low investment. Germany will have to make significant progress in the field of AI. In 2024, software ownership amounted to only 2.1% of added value. This is considerably lower than the average of 6.3%. France tops the list with 11.0%, followed by Austria (8.3%) and Sweden (7.6%).

Making production processes more efficient and reducing staff shortages

Artificial intelligence can make manufacturing companies more efficient. Improving the efficiency of production processes is crucial in order to compensate for the shortage of personnel. Business processes still require countless manual actions at most manufacturing companies. Much of that work can be done more efficiently with further automation and AI, for example, by using self-learning robots. The trend towards smaller and more diverse orders ("high-mix, low-volume"), in particular, requires IT solutions that enable more autonomous production processes. The smart application of AI also makes manufacturing processes faster, higher quality and more reliable.

High value for core manufacturing processes

The number of successful industrial AI use cases is growing by the day. Manufacturers are also increasingly using machine and deep learning models in core processes. For example, for AI-assisted quality checks in the production line, or shortening changeover times through AI-driven machine setting and automatic tool selection. Siemens has reduced automation costs at its Erlangen plant by 90% in certain assembly steps by deploying AI-driven robots that pick up various parts and materials and place them in automated assembly lines. There are also numerous AI applications with potential in logistics processes, for the marketing and sales departments and in service provision. Think of optimising ordering and inventory management, automated answering of customer questions with chatbots and generating optimal pricing and quotations without human intervention.

Greatest gains to be made outside the core process

The greatest potential of (Gen)AI is not in the core process of production itself. This is already highly automated with machines and robots. There is still a lot to be gained in the surrounding processes, such as logistics processes and service provision, but also content creation and analysis applications for departments such as marketing and sales, HR, IT, finance and legal. Concrete examples are:

  • Making production planning more efficient,
  • Translating a product design into processing steps faster and more accurately,
  • Better prediction of wear and tear and malfunctioning machines,
  • Optimising logistics and inventory management and pricing,
  • Autonomous transport systems between warehouse and production line,
  • Automated ordering and quoting processes for standard products,
  • AR (Augmented Reality) applications that support technicians in real time,
  • Automatic documentation via speech-to-text for work instructions and training,
  • Automated answering of customer questions with chatbots,
  • Automated code writing for machine and robot drivers,
  • AI support in the development of new products and processes.

This increases productivity, while employees have more time for work where their knowledge adds the most value.

Smart AI applications are expected to make hardware product development (engineering) much faster and more successful by generating design proposals based on accumulated knowledge. With its strategic partnership with AI developer Mistral, the role of AI at ASML is shifting from supporting software applications to becoming integral to its engineering, production processes, and customer solutions. ASML now deploys various AI agents, drawing on different technologies and serving a wide range of use cases.

Another example is BMW, which uses GenAI in production engineering with NVIDIA. BMW created a synthetic dataset of more than 800,000 images to train vision models for assembly tasks. This reduced the time to develop and implement new quality control models by more than two-thirds.

Many benefits for manufacturing companies

The potential benefits of AI applications for the manufacturing industry can be summarised as follows:

  • Increased efficiency: By automating repetitive tasks and allowing machines to adjust themselves, AI speeds up production, shortens lead times, and significantly reduces errors.
  • Reduced costs: Predictive analytics can reduce the costs of material waste, energy consumption, and maintenance and downtime.
  • Higher quality and fewer rejects: Product quality improves. Vision systems, sensors and deep-learning models detect deviations early, after which machines automatically correct and reduce rejects.
  • Better decision-making: AI's ability to process large amounts of data in real time and simulate scenarios via digital twins enables more informed decisions and allows for better pre-assessment of risks.
  • Greater safety: In addition, AI-controlled robots, cobots and AR-supported instructions increase safety by taking over heavy or dangerous work.
  • Faster innovation: Another advantage is the acceleration in design and development processes. AI facilitates the creation of virtual prototypes and shortens the time-to-market through simulations.
  • More sustainable production: Finally, AI contributes to sustainability by using energy and materials more efficiently and planning maintenance smarter, which makes machines last longer and reduces the environmental impact.

Good data infrastructure is the foundation for success

Sound data infrastructure is the basis for success. Companies that are more digitised can implement AI faster and therefore reap its benefits sooner. Many industrial companies would therefore do well to make their processes more transparent, clean up data and make it more consistent, and to link IT and operational technologies (OT) to ensure reliability. The better the data is unlocked and structured, the more effectively AI can make connections and create value.

Strategic vision from executives and knowledge building are needed to embed AI in work processes

AI requires a clear strategic vision from company directors because the technology brings about a system change that can cause resistance from employees. This is also shown by research that shows that 95% of GenAI pilots fail because organisations try to avoid that friction. However, frontrunners show that friction can be removed with vision and management involvement, especially when AI is not used as a generic tool but is deeply embedded in work processes. Staff are often reluctant to change their existing way of working, do not always have experience with AI and are often concerned about (future) job losses. However, the latter risk seems limited in manufacturing. Continuous staff shortages suggest large-scale layoffs are unlikely. Automation also comes largely at the expense of less competitive activities and less popular activities. In addition, employees are focusing more on the development of products and manufacturing processes and less on the manufacturing process itself.

Building knowledge, starting small and creating scale

Knowledge building is also crucial. Employees need to become familiar with AI step by step so they can recognise its added value and use it more effectively. By starting small, knowledge can be gained in an accessible way, such as with prompting for office-based tasks. In addition, collaboration and consolidation help to create the scale required not only to support digital transformation, but to cope with rising competitive pressure and growing complexity in manufacturing processes, as well as staff shortages and regulatory demands.
